structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
# Description of 3M Company Form 10-K Annual Report (Fiscal Year 2022)

## Main Points Covered:

**Document Overview:**
- 3M Company's Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022
- Filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
- 3M is a Delaware-incorporated diversified technology company established in 1902

**Company Information:**
- Headquarters: 3M Center, St. Paul, Minnesota
- Stock ticker: MMM, traded on New York Stock Exchange and other exchanges
- Classified as a large accelerated filer
- Market value of voting stock: approximately $63.3 billion as of January 31, 2023
- Outstanding shares: 550.5 million as of January 31, 2023

**Business Structure:**
- Operates four reportable business segments: Safety and Industrial; Transportation and Electronics; Health Care; and Consumer
- Diversified technology company with global presence
- Leading manufacturer in many of its served markets
- Announced intention to spin off Health Care business as separate public company (July 2022)

**Financial and Operational Disclosures:**
- Comprehensive financial statements including consolidated statements of income, comprehensive income, balance sheet, changes in equity, and cash flows
- Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) covering overview, results of operations, business segment performance, geographic performance, critical accounting estimates, and financial condition
- Detailed notes to consolidated financial statements covering accounting policies, revenue, acquisitions, goodwill, restructuring, taxes, debt, pensions, derivatives, and other financial matters
- Internal control assessments and auditor reports
- Risk factors and legal proceedings disclosures

structure.title: Business
structure.summary:
# Description of 3M Company Annual Report on Form 10-K (Year Ended December 31, 2022)

## Main Points Covered:

**Company Overview and Business Structure:**
- 3M Company, incorporated in 1929, is a diversified technology company with global operations in over 70 countries
- Operates four business segments: Safety and Industrial, Transportation and Electronics, Health Care, and Consumer
- Approximately 60% of revenues derived from outside the United States
- Employs approximately 92,000 people globally (37,000 in U.S., 55,000 internationally)

**Business Segments and Operations:**
- Safety and Industrial: abrasives, adhesives, personal safety, electrical products
- Transportation and Electronics: automotive materials, display systems, reflective signage
- Health Care: medical solutions, dental products, filtration systems (planned spin-off announced July 2022)
- Consumer: bandages, cleaning products, stationery, home improvement items

**Key Resources and Capabilities:**
- Strong focus on human capital development, diversity, equity, and inclusion
- Extensive patent and trademark portfolio providing competitive advantages
- Global manufacturing presence with 61 facilities in U.S. and 83 internationally
- Supply chain management addressing raw material inflation and geopolitical disruptions

**Major Risk Factors:**
- Global economic, political, and geopolitical uncertainties affecting operations
- PFAS-related liabilities and regulatory compliance (announced exit from PFAS manufacturing by end of 2025)
- Cybersecurity and information technology risks
- Product liability and legal proceedings, including Aearo Technologies bankruptcy proceedings
- Foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations
- Supply chain vulnerabilities and raw material cost pressures
- Planned Health Care business spin-off execution risks

**Financial and Capital Matters:**
- Stock repurchase program ($10 billion authorization with no end date)
- Dividend payments of $1.49 per share in 2022
- Credit rating of A1 (Moody's) and A+ (S&P)
- Pension and postretirement plan obligations subject to market risks

**Regulatory and Compliance:**
- Subject to extensive government regulations including FDA, environmental laws, and trade compliance
- Environmental capital expenditures of $317 million in 2022; projected $646 million for 2023-2024
- Compliance with Foreign Corrupt Practices Act and anti-bribery laws

nodes.title: Risk Factors
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Legal and Regulatory Risks
- 3M is defending multiple PFAS-related lawsuits and regulatory proceedings across various jurisdictions, including Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) multi-district litigation
- The company recorded a $897 million pre-tax charge in Q1 2018 for Minnesota groundwater PFAS settlement
- Exposure to international, federal, state, and local laws and regulations covering product liability, antitrust, intellectual property, environmental/health/safety, tax, anti-bribery, FDA compliance, and healthcare program regulations
- Potential consequences include fines, penalties, criminal/civil sanctions, business limitations, and litigation expenses

## Product and Market Risks
- Demand affected by competitive conditions, customer preferences, pricing strategies, and disruptive technologies (AI, blockchain, analytics)
- Growth dependent on successful new product development and market acceptance
- Supply chain vulnerabilities including material shortages, cost fluctuations, logistics disruptions, and manufacturing site disruptions

## Cybersecurity and Technology Risks
- Exposure to cyberattacks, data breaches, and security vulnerabilities in IT systems and infrastructure
- Risks from third-party vendors and remote work arrangements
- Potential consequences include operational disruption, intellectual property loss, regulatory investigations, and reputational damage

## Business Operations and Strategic Risks
- Risks from acquisitions, divestitures, and strategic alliances
- Operational execution challenges including productivity improvements and ERP system implementation
- Pension and postretirement plan exposure to financial market risks
- Credit rating concerns and potential impact on borrowing costs

## Specific Corporate Actions
- Aearo Technologies subsidiaries' voluntary Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings related to Combat Arms earplugs and respirator products
- Planned spin-off of Health Care business with associated risks and uncertainties

## Properties
- 3M operates 61 manufacturing facilities in 29 U.S. states and 83 facilities in 28 countries
- Headquarters and research laboratories located in St. Paul, Minnesota

nodes.title: Unresolved Staff Comments
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers three main sections:

1. **Risks Related to Aearo Entities' Chapter 11 Bankruptcy Proceedings**: Details 3M's subsidiaries' voluntary chapter 11 filing on July 26, 2022, to establish a trust addressing liabilities from Dual-Ended Combat Arms Version 2 earplugs and respirator products. The section outlines associated risks including legal uncertainties, potential reputational damage, impacts to liquidity and operations, and the unpredictability of litigation outcomes and financial impacts.

2. **Risks Related to Health Care Business Spin-off**: Describes 3M's announced plan to spin off its Health Care business into a separate public company as a tax-free transaction (intended for U.S. federal income tax purposes). The section identifies risks including failure to meet conditions for completion, potential delays, unrealized benefits, business disruption, management distraction, talent retention challenges, and the possibility of unexpected tax liabilities if the transaction fails to qualify for tax-free treatment.

3. **Unresolved Staff Comments and Properties**: Notes there are no unresolved staff comments and provides information about 3M's physical infrastructure, including 61 manufacturing facilities across 29 U.S. states and 83 facilities in 28 countries, with corporate headquarters and research laboratories in St. Paul, Minnesota.

## Risk Factors
1. **Aearo Chapter 11 Bankruptcy Proceedings**: Details 3M's wholly-owned subsidiary Aearo Technologies' voluntary chapter 11 filing on July 26, 2022, to establish a trust addressing liabilities related to Combat Arms Version 2 earplugs and respirator products. Outlines associated risks including legal uncertainties, reputational impacts, liquidity concerns, and operational costs.

2. **Health Care Business Spin-off**: Describes 3M's announced plan to spin off its Health Care business into a separate public company as a tax-free transaction. Identifies risks including failure to meet conditions, management distraction, talent retention challenges, and potential tax liability if the transaction fails to qualify for tax-free treatment.

## Corporate Information

- **Legal Proceedings**: References detailed legal matter discussions in financial statements (Note 16).

- **Mine Safety Disclosures**: Notes compliance with Dodd-Frank Act requirements regarding mine safety violations, with details provided in Exhibit 95.

structure.title: Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations
structure.summary:
# Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations - Key Points

## Overview and Structure
- 3M is a diversified global manufacturer and technology innovator operating in four business segments: Safety and Industrial; Transportation and Electronics; Health Care; and Consumer
- MD&A is organized into eight sections covering operations, segments, geography, accounting estimates, and financial condition
- Effective Q1 2022, 3M changed its segment operating performance measures and non-GAAP adjustments

## Major Business Impacts and Challenges
- **COVID-19 Pandemic**: Continued impacts on demand and global supply chains, with mixed effects across different product lines
- **Russia-Ukraine Conflict**: Suspended Russian operations (less than 1% of 2021 sales) and experienced supply chain disruptions
- **Belgium Manufacturing Facility**: Experienced operational interruptions at Zwijndrecht site; received agreement to restart operations in June 2022
- **Litigation Costs**: 2022 costs of $2.3 billion pre-tax, including $1.3 billion for Combat Arms Earplugs and $355 million for PFAS-related matters
- **PFAS Exit**: Committed to exit PFAS manufacturing by end of 2025 with $0.8 billion impairment charge in 2022
- **Food Safety Divestiture**: Completed split-off with Neogen in Q3 2022, generating $2.7 billion pre-tax gain

## Financial Performance Summary
- **2022 Results**: Net sales declined 3.2% to $34.2 billion; operating income margin decreased to 19.1% from 20.8%; diluted EPS of $10.18
- **2021 Results**: Net sales increased 9.9% to $35.4 billion; operating income margin of 20.8%; diluted EPS of $10.12
- **Key Headwinds**: Raw material inflation, logistics costs, manufacturing productivity challenges, and foreign currency headwinds (3.9% negative impact in 2022)
- **Offsetting Factors**: Strong pricing actions, spending discipline, restructuring benefits, and organic growth of 1.2% in 2022

## Business Segment Performance
- **Safety and Industrial** (33.9% of sales): Organic growth of 1.0% offset by 4.2% currency translation; margins pressured by litigation costs
- **Transportation and Electronics** (26.0% of sales): Organic growth of 1.2% impacted by weak consumer electronics and semiconductor constraints; PFAS exit charges affected margins
- **Health Care** (24.6% of sales): Organic growth of 3.2% with Food Safety divestiture impact; margins pressured by inflation and manufacturing headwinds
- **Consumer** (15.5% of sales): Organic decline of 0.9% due to consumer spending pullback and retail inventory reductions

## Geographic Performance
- **Americas**: 53.8% of sales, organic growth of 2.6%; strong performance in Mexico, Canada, and Brazil
- **Asia Pacific**: 28.9% of sales, organic growth of 0.3%; China declined 3% organically; currency translation negative 6.5%
- **EMEA**: 17.3% of sales, organic decline of 0.6%; currency translation negative 9.8%

## Operating Expenses and Profitability
- **Cost of Sales**: Increased to 56.2% of sales (from 53.2%) due to raw material inflation, logistics costs, and litigation charges
- **SG&A**: Increased to 26.5% of sales (from 20.4%) primarily due to litigation costs and divestiture-related charges
- **R&D**: Decreased slightly to 5.4% of sales; continued investment in innovation and disruptive technologies
- **Goodwill Impairment**: $271 million charge related to PFAS manufacturing exit

## Critical Accounting Estimates
- **Legal Proceedings**: Complex assessments of litigation liabilities, particularly for Combat Arms Earplugs and PFAS matters
- **Pension and Postretirement Obligations**: Discount rates and expected return on assets subject to market volatility; 2022 discount rate increased to 5.18% from 2.89%
- **Goodwill Impairment**: Annual testing completed; no impairment indicated except for Advanced Materials unit related to PFAS exit
- **Long-Lived Assets**: Impairment testing for PFAS manufacturing exit based on discounted cash flow models
- **Income Tax Uncertainties**: Complex tax positions across multiple jurisdictions; effective tax rate of 9.6% in 2022 (benefited from Food Safety split-off structure)

## Financial Condition and Liquidity
- **Strong Balance Sheet**: Cash and equivalents of $3.9 billion; total debt of $15.9 billion; net debt of $12.0 billion
- **Working Capital**: Decreased $1.2 billion to $5.2 billion; inventory increased $387 million
- **Credit Rating**: A1 (Moody's, stable outlook); A+ (S&P, CreditWatch negative)
- **Dividend**: 65th consecutive year of increases; Q1 2023 dividend of $1.50 per share (1% increase)
- **Share Repurchases**: $1.5 billion in 2022; $4.2 billion remaining under authorization

## Cash Flow Analysis
- **Operating Cash Flow**: $5.6 billion in 2022 (down from $7.5 billion in 2021) due to lower net income and working capital changes
- **Investing Cash Flow**: $(1.0) billion; capital expenditures of $1.7 billion; expected 2023 capex of $1.5-1.8 billion
- **Financing Cash Flow**: $(5.4) billion; debt repayments of $1.2 billion; dividends of $3.4 billion
- **Free Cash Flow**: $3.8 billion in 2022 (66% conversion rate) versus $5.9 billion in 2021 (99% conversion rate)

## Risk Management and Market Exposure
- **Foreign Exchange Risk**: Hedges approximately 12-36 months of exposure; 2022 currency headwinds of $271 million on operating income
- **Interest Rate Risk**: Mix of fixed and floating rate debt; $800 million notional interest rate swaps
- **Commodity Price Risk**: Managed through negotiated supply contracts and price protection agreements
- **Liquidity**: $5 billion commercial paper program available; strong access to capital markets

## Forward-Looking Considerations
- Continued investment in growth, productivity, and sustainability initiatives
- Pension contributions expected at $100-200 million in 2023
- Ongoing monitoring of geopolitical risks, supply chain normalization, and market conditions
- Health Care business spin-off announced for future separation as independent public company

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document is Item 7 of 3M's Annual Report on Form 10-K, containing Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) of Financial Condition and Results of Operations. The main points covered include:

## Structure and Overview
- Eight-section MD&A framework covering Overview, Results of Operations, Performance by Business Segment, Performance by Geographic Area, Critical Accounting Estimates, New Accounting Pronouncements, Financial Condition and Liquidity, and Financial Instruments
- 3M's organizational structure with four operating business segments: Safety and Industrial; Transportation and Electronics; Health Care; and Consumer

## Key Business Developments
- Changes to segment reporting and non-GAAP measures effective Q1 2022
- Impact of COVID-19 pandemic on operations and demand
- Russia-Ukraine conflict effects, including suspension of Russian operations in March 2022
- Manufacturing interruptions at Zwijndrecht, Belgium facility with ongoing regulatory compliance efforts
- Significant litigation costs totaling $2.3 billion pre-tax in 2022, including Combat Arms Earplugs and PFAS-related matters
- Split-off of Food Safety Division in September 2022 generating $2.7 billion pre-tax gain
- Commitment to exit PFAS manufacturing by end of 2025

## Financial Performance Analysis

- Sales and operating income by business segment and geographic area
- Discussion of operating expenses including cost of sales, SG&A, R&D, and business divestiture gains

## Financial Condition Items
- Pension and postretirement plan funding status (96% funded worldwide)
- Currency risk management strategies
- Stock repurchase program activity ($1.5 billion in 2022)
- Dividend increase announcement (65th consecutive year)
- Expected pension contributions and expense projections for 2023

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document from 3M's Management Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) covers the company's financial results of operations and business segment performance. The main points include:

## Results of Operations
- **Operating Expenses Analysis**: Details changes in cost of sales, SG&A, R&D, and other operating metrics as percentages of net sales between 2022 and 2021
- **Cost of Sales**: Increased due to PFAS litigation costs, higher raw materials and logistics costs, manufacturing productivity challenges, and Belgium operations shutdown, partially offset by price increases
- **SG&A Expenses**: Rose significantly due to Combat Arms Earplugs litigation charges (~$1.2 billion), PFAS manufacturing exit costs, Russia exit costs, and restructuring charges
- **R&D Expenses**: Slightly decreased as a percentage of sales; company continues investing in product development and disruptive innovations
- **Business Divestitures**: 3M recorded a $2.7 billion pre-tax gain from the Food Safety business split-off and combination with Neogen Corporation
- **Goodwill Impairment**: Recorded impairment charge related to PFAS manufacturing exit commitment

## Other Financial Items
- Interest expense decreased in 2022 due to debt maturities and interest income on invested cash
- Effective tax rate was 9.6% in 2022 versus 17.8% in 2021
- Income from unconsolidated subsidiaries remained minimal at $11 million

## Business Segments
- 3M operates four reportable business segments: Safety and Industrial; Transportation and Electronics; Health Care; and Consumer
- The company updated its segment operating performance measurement methodology effective Q1 2022

# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers the Consumer Business segment performance and geographic area analysis for 3M Company, along with critical accounting estimates.

## Main Points:

**Consumer Business Segment (15.5% of consolidated sales):**
- 2022 sales declined 3.9% to $5.3 billion, driven by reduced consumer discretionary spending and retailer inventory reductions
- Organic sales decreased 0.9%, with mixed performance across product categories (growth in stationery/office and home care; decline in home improvement)
- Operating income margins compressed due to increased raw material, logistics, and manufacturing costs, partially offset by pricing actions and cost discipline
- 2021 showed strong growth of 10.8% with broad-based demand across all product categories

**Geographic Area Performance:**
- Total company employees decreased from 95,000 to 92,000
- Capital spending increased to $1.75 billion in 2022
- Net property, plant and equipment totaled $9.18 billion
- Americas region represents the largest investment area with $1.32 billion in capital spending
- 3M is strategically increasing manufacturing investments to align production capability with regional sales

**Critical Accounting Estimates:**
- Document identifies key areas requiring management judgment: legal proceedings assessments and pension/postretirement obligations
- Emphasizes the inherent uncertainties in estimating contingent liabilities and defined benefit plan assumptions
- References detailed accounting policies and methodologies in financial statement notes

The document concludes with a reference to new accounting pronouncements detailed in the financial statements notes.

# Description of Partial Document: Financial Condition and Liquidity

This section of 3M's financial document provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial health, liquidity position, and cash flow management. The main points covered include:

## Financial Flexibility and Capital Deployment
- 3M's strong business model and free cash flow capability enable flexible capital deployment
- Primary capital priorities include organic growth investments (R&D, capital expenditures), portfolio management through acquisitions/divestitures, and shareholder returns via dividends and share repurchases

## Liquidity Profile
- Strong liquidity maintained through cash on hand and commercial paper issuances
- $5 billion commercial paper program available with up to 397-day maturity
- No commercial paper outstanding at year-end 2022 or 2021

## Debt Management
- Total debt decreased from $17.4 billion (2021) to $15.9 billion (2022)
- Credit ratings: A1 (Moody's, stable outlook) and A+ (S&P, CreditWatch negative)
- Staggered debt maturity profile ensures manageable refinancing needs
- $18 billion medium-term notes program (Series F) with approximately $17.6 billion issued
- LIBOR transition addressed for debt securities and contracts

## Cash and Liquidity Positions
- Cash, equivalents, and marketable securities: $3.9 billion (2022) vs. $4.8 billion (2021)
- Net debt (non-GAAP): $12.0 billion (2022) vs. $12.6 billion (2021)
- Working capital: $5.2 billion (2022) vs. $6.4 billion (2021)

## Cash Flow Analysis
- Operating cash flow: $5.6 billion (2022) vs. $7.5 billion (2021)
- Capital expenditures: $1.7 billion (2022); expected $1.5-1.8 billion for 2023
- Free cash flow: $3.8 billion (2022) vs. $5.9 billion (2021)
- Financing activities included $1.5 billion stock repurchases and $3.4 billion dividend payments

## Material Cash Obligations
- Future interest payments on long-term debt: approximately $6 billion
- Tax obligations, lease commitments, and contingencies detailed in referenced notes
- Limited unconditional purchase commitments with vendors

nodes.title: Financial Instruments
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers 3M Company's financial risk management strategies and market risk exposures. The main points include:

**Financial Instruments Overview:**
- The company uses foreign exchange forward contracts, options, and swaps to hedge currency fluctuations and protect cash flows in foreign currencies
- Interest rate risks are managed through a combination of fixed and floating rate debt, with potential use of interest rate swaps
- Commodity price risks are managed via negotiated supply contracts and price protection agreements

**Market Risk Management Structure:**
- Senior management oversees risk management and derivative activities
- The company establishes financial risk policies, derivative utilization guidelines, and control procedures
- Risk monitoring includes credit approvals, credit limits, and selection of major international banks as counterparties

**Foreign Exchange Risk:**
- The company hedges exposure to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations through forward and option contracts designated as cash flow hedges (up to 36 months)
- Non-designated foreign exchange contracts offset impacts on non-functional currency items and intercompany financing
- As of December 31, 2022: $3.2 billion in designated hedges and $2.8 billion in non-designated contracts
- €2.4 billion in foreign currency denominated debt used for net investment hedges

**Interest Rate Risk:**
- The company manages interest rate volatility through mixed fixed and floating rate debt portfolios
- Interest rate swaps designated as fair value hedges are used to exchange fixed and floating interest payments
- $800 million gross notional amount of interest rate swaps as of December 31, 2022

**Commodity Price Risk:**
- Managed through negotiated supply contracts and price protection agreements

structure.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers 3M Company's financial instruments and market risk management practices. The main points include:

**Financial Instruments Overview:**
- The company uses foreign exchange forward contracts, options, and swaps to hedge currency fluctuations and protect non-functional currency items
- Interest rate risks are managed through a mix of fixed and floating rate debt, with potential use of interest rate swaps
- Commodity price risks are managed through negotiated supply contracts and price protection agreements

**Market Risk Exposures:**
- Foreign exchange rates risk affecting net investments in foreign subsidiaries and cash flow translations
- Interest rate volatility risk related to existing and future debt issuances
- Commodity price fluctuations impacting production costs

**Hedging Instruments and Amounts (as of December 31, 2022):**
- Foreign exchange contracts designated as hedges: $3.2 billion notional amount
- Foreign exchange contracts not designated as hedges: $2.8 billion notional amount
- Foreign currency denominated debt: €2.4 billion principal amount
- Interest rate swaps: $800 million notional amount

**Risk Management Framework:**
- Senior management oversight of derivative activities and risk policies
- Credit risk monitoring through counterparty selection and credit limits
- Value at Risk (VaR) analysis using Monte Carlo simulation at 95% confidence level

**Value at Risk Analysis Results (2022):**
- Foreign exchange: potential adverse impact of $315 million or positive impact of $314 million on after-tax earnings
- Interest rates: potential adverse impact of $18 million or positive impact of $18 million
- Commodity prices: potential adverse impact of $5 million or positive impact of $7 million
- Sensitivity analysis showing 1% component price change = ~$85 million impact; 10% energy price change = ~$45 million impact

# Description of Partial Document

This section of a 3M Company financial document covers three main areas:

1. **Value at Risk (VaR) Analysis**: Details the Company's annual assessment of sensitivity to currency rates, interest rates, and commodity prices using Monte Carlo simulation at a 95% confidence level. The analysis presents potential adverse and positive impacts on after-tax earnings for 2022 and 2021, showing:
   - Foreign exchange exposure of $315 million adverse impact
   - Interest rate exposure of $18 million adverse impact
   - Commodity price exposure of $5 million adverse impact
   - Additional analysis of global component/material purchases and energy costs with specific sensitivity metrics

2. **Management's Responsibility for Financial Reporting**: Outlines management's accountability for financial information integrity, preparation in accordance with GAAP, and establishment of internal control systems designed to safeguard assets, ensure accurate record-keeping, and maintain ethical standards.

3. **Management's Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting**: Confirms that management has assessed the Company's internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2022, using the COSO framework, concluding that controls are effective. Notes that PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP independently audited these controls and issued an unqualified opinion on their effectiveness.

nodes.title: Management's Responsibility for Financial Reporting
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains management's statements regarding financial reporting responsibilities and internal controls for 3M Company. The main points covered include:

1. **Management's Financial Reporting Responsibility**: Management affirms its responsibility for the integrity and objectivity of financial information, confirming that financial statements are prepared in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) in the United States, with estimates based on management judgment where necessary.

2. **Internal Control System**: Management has established and maintains a comprehensive system of internal control over financial reporting designed to:
   - Safeguard assets
   - Ensure accurate recording of transactions in books and records
   - Verify proper implementation of policies and procedures by qualified personnel
   - Ensure financial statements are properly prepared and fairly presented

3. **Control Framework and Ethical Standards**: The internal control system is supported by written policies, including business conduct policies that require employees to maintain high ethical standards. Internal auditors continuously review the accounting and control systems.

4. **Management Assessment of Internal Controls**: Management assessed the Company's internal control over financial reporting using the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations (COSO) Integrated Framework (2013) and concluded that as of December 31, 2022, the internal controls are effective.

5. **Independent Audit Verification**: PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP, an independent registered public accounting firm, audited the internal control over financial reporting and issued an unqualified opinion confirming its effectiveness as of December 31, 2022.

nodes.title: Management's Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains the financial reporting governance and audit sections from 3M Company's annual report, specifically covering:

## Main Points:

1. **Management's Responsibility for Financial Reporting** - Outlines management's accountability for the integrity and objectivity of financial information, prepared in accordance with U.S. GAAP, and the establishment of internal control systems to safeguard assets and ensure accurate financial reporting.

2. **Internal Control System Overview** - Describes the company's system of internal controls supported by written policies, business conduct guidelines, and continuous internal auditor reviews designed to maintain high ethical standards.

3. **Management's Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting** - Documents management's assessment of internal control effectiveness as of December 31, 2022, based on the COSO Integrated Framework (2013), concluding that controls are effective.

4. **Independent Auditor's Report** - Presents PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP's audit opinions on:
   - The consolidated financial statements for 2022 and 2021 (presenting fairly the company's financial position and results)
   - The effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2022

5. **Audit Methodology and Standards** - Explains the auditing procedures conducted in accordance with PCAOB standards to assess risks of material misstatement and evaluate internal control design and effectiveness.

6. **Definition and Limitations of Internal Control** - Defines internal control over financial reporting and acknowledges its inherent limitations in preventing or detecting all misstatements.

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains the **Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm** and **Consolidated Statement of Income** for 3M Company for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2022, 2021, and 2020.

## Main Points Covered:

**Auditor's Report Section:**
- Unqualified opinions on 3M's consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2022
- Confirmation that financial statements present fairly the company's financial position and results of operations in accordance with U.S. GAAP
- Description of audit procedures and standards followed (PCAOB standards)
- Definition and limitations of internal control over financial reporting

**Critical Audit Matters:**
1. **Legal Proceedings** - Significant judgment required in assessing likelihood and estimating losses for legal claims
2. **Tax-free Determination of Food Safety Division Split-Off** - Complex tax determination involving a Reverse Morris Trust structure and IRS private letter ruling
3. **Property, Plant and Equipment and Goodwill Impairment Assessments** - $0.8 billion impairment charge recorded for Advanced Materials Division related to PFAS manufacturing exit, involving fair value estimates using discounted cash flow models

**Consolidated Statement of Income:**
- Net sales of $34.2 billion in 2022 (down from $35.4 billion in 2021)
- Operating income of $6.5 billion in 2022
- Net income attributable to 3M of $5.8 billion in 2022
- Diluted earnings per share of $10.18 in 2022
- Significant goodwill impairment expense of $271 million in 2022

## New Accounting Pronouncements

- ASU No. 2021-08 (effective January 1, 2023): Requires proper accounting for contract assets and liabilities in business combinations
- ASU No. 2022-04 (effective January 1, 2023): Requires disclosure of supplier finance program obligations with no impact to financial results

## Revenue (Note 2)
- Details contract balances and deferred revenue primarily from software licenses
- Reports operating lease revenue from medical devices ($577-586 million annually)
- Provides comprehensive disaggregated revenue by business segment and geographic region for 2020-2022
- Shows total company net sales of $34.2 billion (2022), $35.4 billion (2021), and $32.2 billion (2020)
- Breaks down revenue across four business groups: Safety and Industrial, Transportation and Electronics, Health Care, and Consumer

## Acquisitions and Divestitures (Note 3)
- Reports no acquisitions closed in 2020-2022
- Details 2022 divestitures including:
  - Sale of Western European floor products business
  - Announced spin-off of Health Care business as separate public company (expected completion by end of 2023)
  - Completed split-off of Food Safety Division to Neogen Corporation in September 2022, generating a $2.7 billion pre-tax gain through a Reverse Morris Trust structure

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains Notes 3 and 4 from 3M's financial statements, covering acquisitions, divestitures, goodwill, and intangible assets.

## Main Points:

**NOTE 3 - Acquisitions and Divestitures:**
- 3M had no acquisitions that closed in 2022, 2021, or 2020
- **2022 Divestitures:** Completed sale of Western European floor products business; announced spin-off of Health Care business as separate public company (expected completion by end of 2023); completed split-off of Food Safety Division to Neogen Corporation in a Reverse Morris Trust transaction, resulting in a $2.7 billion pre-tax gain
- **2020 Divestitures:** Sold advanced ballistic-protection business to Avon Rubber for $86 million; sold Drug Delivery Division to Altaris Capital Partners for $617 million, resulting in $387 million pre-tax gain; sold small dermatology products business for immaterial proceeds
- No divestitures closed in 2021

**NOTE 4 - Goodwill and Intangible Assets:**
- Goodwill balance decreased from $13.5 billion (December 31, 2021) to $12.8 billion (December 31, 2022)
- Recorded $271 million goodwill impairment in 2022, primarily related to Advanced Materials reporting unit due to PFAS manufacturing exit commitment
- No goodwill recorded from acquisitions in 2022 or 2021
- Total finite-lived intangible assets (net) decreased from $4.6 billion to $4.1 billion
- Non-amortizable intangible assets (primarily tradenames) totaled $617 million as of December 31, 2022

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers 3M Company's 2020 restructuring actions and supplemental income statement information:

## Main Points:

**2020 Restructuring Actions:**
- Two separate restructuring initiatives were approved in Q2 2020

**Divestiture-Related Restructuring:**
- Triggered by the sale of substantially all of the drug delivery business
- Addressed corporate functional costs and manufacturing footprint adjustments
- Affected approximately 1,300 positions worldwide
- Generated a $55 million pre-tax charge ($42M in cost of sales, $12M in SG&A, $1M in R&D)
- Included both employee-related costs ($32M) and asset-related/other costs ($23M)
- Tracking of accrued balances through June 30, 2021, with completion in Q3 2021

**Other Restructuring (COVID-19 Related):**
- Addressed structural enterprise costs and operations in certain end markets due to COVID-19 pandemic impacts
- Affected approximately 400 positions worldwide
- Generated a $58 million pre-tax charge distributed across cost of sales ($13M), SG&A ($37M), and R&D ($8M)
- Impacted all business segments with varying charges
- Primarily employee-related costs ($35M) with asset-related charges ($23M)
- Largely completed by Q2 2021

**Supplemental Income Statement Information (Note 6):**
- Details on other expense/income components including interest expense, interest income, and pension/postretirement benefit costs for 2020-2022
- Includes information on early debt extinguishment charges

nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers two main topics:

## 1. Federal, State and Foreign Tax (Note 10)

**Effective Tax Rate Reconciliation:**
- Presents a reconciliation of the company's effective worldwide tax rate for 2022, 2021, and 2020
- Shows the statutory U.S. tax rate of 21.0% adjusted by various factors to arrive at effective rates of 9.6%, 17.8%, and 19.7% respectively
- Key adjustments include the Food Safety divestiture (8.4% benefit in 2022), FDII benefits, R&D credits, and GILTI provisions
- The significant 8.2 percentage point decrease from 2021 to 2022 was primarily driven by the tax-efficient structure of the Food Safety business split-off

**Tax Contingencies and Audits:**
- Details the company's ongoing IRS examination status through 2018, with unresolved issues from 2005-2017
- Notes ongoing state and foreign tax audits with no significant proposed adjustments as of December 31, 2022
- Discusses unrecognized tax benefits (UTB) totaling $927 million at year-end 2022, down from $1,071 million in 2021

**Foreign Operations and Repatriation:**
- Describes tax incentives in China, Switzerland, Brazil, and Singapore providing $170 million in benefits in 2022
- Details $16.0 billion in undistributed foreign earnings, with $8.0 billion indefinitely reinvested

## 2. Marketable Securities (Note 11)

- Summarizes the company's investment portfolio including commercial paper, certificates of deposit, and treasury/municipal securities
- Total marketable securities of $261 million as of December 31, 2022
- Provides maturity schedule showing most securities ($238 million) due within one year

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains detailed financial disclosures from a company's financial statements, primarily covering tax matters, marketable securities, debt, and pension plans:

## Main Points Covered:

**Unrecognized Tax Benefits (UTB):**
- Total UTB impact on effective tax rate: $965 million (2022), $1,112 million (2021), and $1,145 million (2020)
- Interest and penalties accrued related to UTB are recognized in tax expense
- Foreign subsidiaries in China, Switzerland, Brazil, and Singapore benefit from reduced tax rates or tax exemptions through specified years
- Company has $16.0 billion in undistributed foreign earnings, with $8.0 billion indefinitely reinvested

**Marketable Securities:**
- Total marketable securities of $261 million (2022) and $228 million (2021)
- Portfolio includes commercial paper, certificates of deposit, U.S. treasury securities, and municipal securities
- Maturity schedule shows most securities due within one year

**Long-Term Debt and Short-Term Borrowings:**
- Total long-term debt of $15,939 million (2022) and $17,347 million (2021)
- Detailed breakdown of medium-term notes, registered notes, and floating rate notes with various maturity dates through 2050
- Fixed-rate debt comprises majority of portfolio at 2.93% effective interest rate
- Company maintains $3.0 billion revolving credit facility and $1.25 billion 364-day credit facility, both undrawn
- Debt covenant requires EBITDA to Interest Ratio of at least 3.0 to 1 (actual ratio: 19 to 1)

**Pension and Postretirement Benefit Plans:**
- Over 75 defined benefit plans across 28 countries
- Primary U.S. pension plan closed to new participants since January 1, 2009
- Postretirement health care and life insurance benefits provided for eligible U.S. employees

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers comprehensive information about 3M Company's pension and postretirement benefit plans, including:

## Key Topics Covered:

**Balance Sheet Recognition:**
- Amounts recognized for qualified/non-qualified pension benefits and postretirement benefits as of December 31, 2022 and 2021
- Current and non-current liabilities and assets related to benefit obligations
- Accumulated other comprehensive income balances including net actuarial losses and prior service costs

**Benefit Obligations and Plan Assets:**
- Accumulated benefit obligations and projected benefit obligations for U.S. and international plans
- Fair value of plan assets for plans with obligations exceeding assets
- Detailed breakdown of pension plan asset allocations by asset class

**Net Periodic Benefit Costs:**
- Components of net periodic benefit costs including service costs, interest costs, expected returns on assets, and amortization of actuarial losses
- Three-year comparison (2020-2022) across different benefit plan categories
- Other comprehensive income impacts from plan changes

**Actuarial Assumptions:**
- Weighted-average discount rates, compensation rate increases, and expected returns on assets
- Methodology for determining discount rates based on high-quality fixed-income instruments
- Changes in mortality improvement scales and their impacts

**Funding and Contributions:**
- Historical and projected pension and postretirement benefit contributions
- Future benefit payment estimates for five-year periods
- Investment strategy and asset management approach

**Plan Asset Details:**
- Fair value measurements using three-level hierarchy for U.S. pension plans, postretirement benefit plans, and international pension plans
- Asset allocation across equities, fixed income, private equity, absolute return, and cash equivalents
- Valuation methodologies for different investment types

**Derivatives:**
- Overview of interest rate swaps, currency swaps, and forward/option contracts used for hedging
- Cash flow hedges for foreign currency and interest rate exposures
- Fair value hedge accounting treatment

nodes.title: Note 14. Derivatives
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers key financial accounting topics related to 3M Company's investment portfolio and derivative instruments:

## Main Points Covered:

1. **Asset Classification and Valuation**
   - Absolute return investments (hedge funds, insurance contracts, derivatives, alternative investments)
   - Insurance contracts valued using cash surrender values
   - Derivative instruments (swaps, bond futures) for risk management
   - International pension plans' Level 3 assets and their changes in fair value

2. **Derivative Instruments and Hedging Strategies**
   - Use of interest rate swaps, currency swaps, and forward/option contracts to manage foreign exchange and interest rate risks
   - Four types of hedging relationships: cash flow hedges, fair value hedges, net investment hedges, and non-designated derivatives

3. **Cash Flow Hedges**
   - Foreign currency forward and option contracts for exchange rate fluctuations
   - Interest rate contracts for forecasted debt issuances
   - Accounting treatment through accumulated other comprehensive income

4. **Fair Value Hedges**
   - Interest rate swaps on fixed-rate debt obligations
   - Mark-to-market gains/losses recorded in interest expense
   - Historical examples of swaps from 2013-2021

5. **Net Investment Hedges**
   - Foreign currency denominated debt and forward contracts to hedge foreign subsidiary investments
   - Gains/losses recorded in cumulative translation within other comprehensive income

6. **Fair Value Measurements and Offsetting**
   - Master netting arrangements with counterparties
   - Gross and net presentation of derivative assets and liabilities
   - Credit risk management and counterparty exposure

7. **Valuation Methodologies**
   - Market and income approaches for financial assets and liabilities
   - Classification levels (Level 1, Level 2) for marketable securities

nodes.title: Note 16. Commitments and Contingencies
nodes.summary:
# Summary of Main Points in Partial Document

## Fair Value Measurements and Financial Instruments
- 3M measures pension and postretirement benefit plan assets at fair value on a recurring basis (at least annually)
- Nonrecurring fair value measurements relate to asset impairments, goodwill impairments, and equity security adjustments
- In 2022, 3M recorded a $109 million charge for Russia operations exit and an $800 million charge for PFAS manufacturing exit
- Long-term debt fair values are estimated using third-party quotes and market prices (Level 2 classification)

## Commitments and Contingencies
- Product warranty liabilities are approximately $48 million as of December 31, 2022
- 3M is involved in numerous legal proceedings including product liability, intellectual property, environmental, and regulatory matters
- The company records legal liabilities when losses are probable and reasonably estimable, following ASC 450 guidance

## Respirator Mask/Asbestos Litigation
- Approximately 4,028 individual claimants with pending lawsuits as of December 31, 2022
- 3M has prevailed in 15 of 16 jury trials over 20+ years
- Accrual of $604 million established for respirator mask/asbestos liabilities (excluding Aearo)
- Insurance receivable of $4 million recorded for recovery

## Environmental Matters and PFAS Litigation
- 3M announced plans to exit all PFAS manufacturing by end of 2025
- Significant regulatory activity in Europe and United States regarding PFAS restrictions
- 3M Belgium committed 571 million euros for Zwijndrecht facility remediation and environmental measures
- Multiple state attorneys general lawsuits pending regarding PFAS contamination
- 3,350 AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam) lawsuits consolidated in federal MDL court in South Carolina
- Environmental liabilities of $592 million recorded for PFAS-related matters as of December 31, 2022

## Product Liability Litigation
- Aearo Technologies Combat Arms Earplugs: approximately 232,000 claimants; 3M committed $1.2 billion to fund bankruptcy trust in July 2022
- Bair Hugger patient warming system: approximately 5,258 lawsuits pending; MDL consolidated in Minnesota federal court
- Multiple bellwether trials conducted with mixed verdicts for 3M

## Securities and Shareholder Litigation
- Securities class action dismissed in September 2021
- Derivative lawsuits pending in Minnesota federal court with motion to dismiss briefed

## Leases
- Operating lease right-of-use assets of $829 million as of December 31, 2022
- Finance lease obligations of $86 million
- Total lease expense of $447 million in 2022

## Stock-Based Compensation
- 32 million shares remaining available for grant under 2016 Long-Term Incentive Plan
- Stock-based compensation expense of $263 million in 2022
- Annual grants made in February using closing stock price on grant date

nodes.title: Legal Proceedings
nodes.summary:
# Description of Legal Proceedings and Environmental Matters Document

## Main Points Covered:

**Legal Proceedings Overview:**
- The company faces numerous claims and lawsuits in the United States and worldwide, including product liability, intellectual property, commercial, antitrust, healthcare program violations, securities, and environmental matters
- The company vigorously defends all litigation and cooperates with government agencies

**Respirator Mask/Asbestos Litigation:**
- Approximately 4,028 individual claimants as of December 31, 2022
- Claims allege personal injury from workplace exposure to asbestos, silica, coal mine dust, and other occupational dusts
- Company has prevailed in 15 of 16 jury trials over 20+ years
- Accrual of $604 million recorded for these liabilities (excluding Aearo)
- Aearo Technologies initiated chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings in July 2022 to establish a trust for related claims

**Environmental Matters and PFAS Litigation:**
- Extensive regulatory and legislative activity regarding per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in the United States and Europe
- Company announced exit from all PFAS manufacturing by end of 2025
- Multiple environmental liabilities recorded, including $592 million for PFAS-related environmental liabilities and $29 million for non-PFAS remediation costs
- Significant litigation in Belgium (3M Belgium facility in Zwijndrecht), Alabama, and multiple U.S. states
- Company committed 571 million euros to address Zwijndrecht facility contamination

**AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam) Litigation:**
- 3,350 lawsuits filed alleging injuries from AFFF use
- Majority consolidated in federal MDL in South Carolina
- Claims include personal injury, property damage, water treatment costs, and natural resource damages

**Product Liability Litigation:**
- Combat Arms Earplugs: Approximately 232,000 claimants; Aearo Entities filed chapter 11; company committed $1.2 billion for trust funding
- Bair Hugger Patient Warming System: Approximately 5,258 lawsuits alleging surgical site infections

**Securities and Shareholder Litigation:**
- Securities class action dismissed in September 2021
- Derivative lawsuits pending regarding PFAS exposure disclosures

**Other Matters:**
- Federal False Claims Act litigation related to KCI subsidiaries
- FCPA compliance investigation related to China-based marketing activities
- Lease expense information provided

nodes.title: Process for Disclosure and Recording of Liabilities Related to Legal Proceedings
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the Company's legal proceedings and contingencies, including:

## Main Points:

1. **Overview of Legal Proceedings**: The Company and its subsidiaries are involved in numerous claims, lawsuits, and regulatory proceedings primarily in the United States, spanning multiple areas including products liability, intellectual property, commercial disputes, antitrust, healthcare program compliance, securities, and environmental matters.

2. **Types of Legal Exposure**: The Company faces various legal challenges related to products it currently or formerly manufactured and sold, as well as regulatory investigations and subpoenas from government agencies in the U.S. and foreign countries.

3. **Accounting Standards for Liability Recording**: The Company follows ASC 450 (Contingencies) guidelines when recording liabilities, recording accruals only when losses are probable and reasonably estimable. When a range exists, the Company records either the most likely estimate or the low end of the range.

4. **Disclosure Requirements**: The Company discloses significant legal proceedings even when liability is not probable or estimable if there is a reasonable possibility of loss occurring.

5. **Uncertainty and Future Impact**: The document emphasizes that litigation involves inherent uncertainties, and unfavorable rulings could result in charges exceeding currently recorded liabilities, potentially having material adverse effects on financial results.

6. **Insurance Receivables Process**: The Company estimates and records insurance receivables based on policy analysis, case law, and experience with similar claims, following ASC 450 and ASC 610-30 guidance.

7. **Specific Litigation Example**: The document begins detailing respirator mask/asbestos litigation with approximately 4,028 individual claimants as of December 31, 2022.

nodes.title: Respirator Mask/Asbestos Litigation
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the Company's legal proceedings and contingency accounting practices:

## Main Points:

1. **Overview of Legal Proceedings**: The Company and its subsidiaries are involved in numerous claims and lawsuits primarily in the United States, including products liability, intellectual property, commercial, antitrust, healthcare-related, securities, and environmental matters. The Company vigorously defends all litigation and cooperates with government agency requests.

2. **Accounting Standards for Legal Liabilities**: The Company follows ASC 450 (Contingencies) when recording liabilities for legal proceedings. Liabilities are recorded when losses are probable and reasonably estimable, using either the most likely estimate or the low end of a range when no single best estimate exists.

3. **Disclosure Requirements**: The Company discloses significant legal proceedings even when liability is not probable or estimable if there is a reasonable possibility of loss. The document acknowledges that litigation uncertainties could result in future charges exceeding currently recorded liabilities.

4. **Insurance Receivables**: The Company estimates and records insurance receivables based on policy analysis, case law, and experience, following ASC 450 and ASC 610-30 guidance.

5. **Respirator Mask/Asbestos Litigation**: A detailed case study showing approximately 4,028 pending individual claimants alleging injuries from asbestos, silica, coal mine dust, or occupational exposures. Key points include:
   - Company's strong trial record (15 of 16 jury verdicts in favor)
   - 2019 settlement of coal mine dust cases for $340 million
   - Increasing proportion of serious injury claims (mesothelioma, malignancies, black lung disease)
   - West Virginia state case seeking unspecified damages and civil penalties

nodes.title: Respirator Mask/Asbestos Liabilities and Insurance Receivables
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section addresses the Company's respirator mask and asbestos-related liabilities and insurance receivables. The main points covered include:

1. **Liability Assessment Process**: The Company conducts comprehensive legal reviews of respirator mask/asbestos liabilities, analyzing claims data including pending claims, claim characteristics, defense costs, and filing trends with assistance from third-party experts.

2. **Factors Affecting Liability Estimates**: Various developments can impact liability estimates, including changes in key assumptions about future claims, trial outcomes, applicable law, and the financial viability of co-defendants and insurers.

3. **2022 Accrual and Payments**: The Company increased its accruals by $31 million in 2022, made payments of $67 million for legal defense and settlements, and maintained a total accrual of $604 million as of December 31, 2022, with an estimation period extending to approximately 2050.

4. **Estimation Challenges**: The Company acknowledges difficulties in estimating the upper range of potential liabilities due to challenges in projecting unasserted claims, multi-defendant litigation structures with joint and several liability considerations, and multiple unpredictable factors.

5. **Insurance Recoveries**: As of December 31, 2022, the Company had $4 million in insurance receivables and continues pursuing coverage from insolvent insurers.

6. **Aearo Technologies Bankruptcy**: In July 2022, Aearo Entities initiated Chapter 11 bankruptcy proceedings to establish a trust funded by the Company to address respirator mask/asbestos claims and Combat Arms earplugs litigation, representing a strategic change in liability management.

nodes.title: Environmental Matters
nodes.summary:
# Description of Environmental Matters Document

## Main Points Covered:

**1. Company's PFAS Phase-Out and Future Commitments**
- 3M phased out manufacturing of PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, and related compounds starting in 2000, completing cessation by 2008
- Company announced in December 2022 plans to exit all PFAS manufacturing and discontinue PFAS use across product portfolio by end of 2025

**2. Global PFAS Regulatory Landscape**
- Accelerating regulatory and legislative activities in the United States, Europe, and internationally
- Increasingly strict restrictions on PFAS in products and manufacturing emissions, with regulations moving toward non-detectable limits
- Significant uncertainty regarding potential costs for remediation and control technologies

**3. European Operations and Compliance Issues**
- 3M Belgium facility in Zwijndrecht facing multiple regulatory challenges, including wastewater discharge restrictions and emissions prohibitions
- Company committed 571 million euros in July 2022 to address PFAS contamination and remediation at Zwijndrecht facility
- Dyneon facility in Germany managing PFOA byproducts from recycling processes
- Nine pending civil litigation actions against 3M Belgium involving soil and water contamination claims

**4. U.S. Federal Regulatory Actions**
- EPA released health advisory levels for various PFAS compounds and is developing national primary drinking water standards
- EPA added PFAS to multiple regulatory lists and is pursuing CERCLA and RCRA designations
- Expanded reporting requirements under TSCA and TRI for PFAS substances

**5. U.S. State-Level Regulatory Activity**
- Multiple states have enacted drinking water standards and product restrictions for PFAS
- States including California, Maine, and Colorado have passed legislation restricting PFAS in consumer products
- 3M involved in litigation challenging certain state regulations in New Jersey, New York, and Michigan

**6. Litigation Related to Historical PFAS Contamination**
- Numerous settlements reached regarding Alabama manufacturing facility contamination, including $35 million settlement with West Morgan-East Lawrence Water Authority and $99 million collaborative agreement with City of Decatur
- Multiple state attorneys general lawsuits pending in federal MDL court regarding AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam) contamination
- Ongoing negotiations with property owners and individual plaintiffs regarding contamination claims

# Description of Environmental Matters Document

**2. Global PFAS Regulatory Landscape**
- Accelerating regulatory and legislative activities in the United States, Europe, and internationally
- Increasingly strict restrictions on PFAS in products and manufacturing emissions, with regulations moving toward non-detectable limits
- Significant uncertainty regarding potential costs for remediation and control technologies

**3. European Regulatory Actions**
- EU REACH restrictions and Stockholm Convention listings affecting PFOA, PFOS, and PFHxS
- EU Food Contaminants Regulation and Drinking Water Directive establishing limits for PFAS in food and drinking water
- Detailed discussion of 3M Belgium's Zwijndrecht facility challenges, including safety measures, wastewater discharge limits, emissions restrictions, and soil remediation obligations
- 3M Belgium's €571 million commitment to address environmental impacts and remediation

**4. United States Federal Regulatory Activity**
- EPA's PFAS Strategic Roadmap and development of health advisory levels
- EPA's process for establishing national primary drinking water standards for PFOA and PFOS
- Addition of PFAS to various EPA lists and proposed CERCLA/RCRA designations
- Expanded reporting requirements under TSCA and TRI

**5. United States State-Level Actions**
- Multiple states establishing drinking water standards and cleanup levels for PFAS
- State restrictions on PFAS in consumer products (textiles, cosmetics, food packaging, furniture)
- Maine's comprehensive ban on intentionally added PFAS effective 2030
- 3M's litigation challenging certain state regulations

**6. PFAS-Related Litigation**
- Resolved and pending claims related to 3M's Decatur, Alabama facility contamination
- Multiple settlements with water authorities, municipalities, and class action plaintiffs
- State attorneys general lawsuits in federal MDL court regarding AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam)
- Civil litigation in Belgium and ongoing investigations in the Netherlands

nodes.title: Europe
nodes.summary:
# Description of Environmental Matters Document

This document covers 3M's environmental compliance and regulatory issues related to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS). The main points include:

## Company Actions and Phase-Out
- 3M phased out manufacturing of certain PFAS compounds (PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS) starting in 2000, completing cessation by 2008
- In December 2022, 3M announced plans to exit all PFAS manufacturing and discontinue PFAS use across its product portfolio by end of 2025

## Regulatory Landscape
- PFAS regulations are accelerating globally, with increasingly strict restrictions on manufacturing emissions and environmental presence
- Regulations are moving toward non-detectable limits and decreasing thresholds in drinking water, soil, and other environmental media

## European Union Regulatory Issues
- The EU is pursuing broad PFAS restrictions under REACH and POPs regulations
- New EU Food Contaminants Regulation (effective January 2023) restricts PFAS in foodstuffs
- EU Drinking Water Directive sets limits of 0.1 µg/L for 20 PFAS compounds

## 3M Belgium Zwijndrecht Facility (Major Focus)
- Historical PFAS contamination at the facility triggered investigations by Flemish authorities
- Multiple regulatory actions including safety measures on wastewater discharge and emissions restrictions
- 3M Belgium committed 571 million euros in remediation and support efforts under a July 2022 agreement with Flemish Government
- Nine civil lawsuits pending against 3M Belgium for soil and water contamination claims
- Company recorded approximately $500 million pre-tax charge in first half of 2022

## United States Federal Regulatory Activity
- EPA released new health advisory levels for various PFAS compounds in June 2022
- EPA is developing national primary drinking water standards for PFOA and PFOS (expected early 2023)
- EPA added PFAS to lists for screening, removal management, and hazardous substance designations
- Expanded reporting requirements under TSCA and TRI for PFAS compounds
- EPA issued test orders requiring health and safety testing for certain PFAS

nodes.title: United States: Federal Activity
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This document outlines federal and state regulatory activities in the United States regarding PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances), particularly PFOA and PFOS, and their implications for 3M Company.

## Federal Activity (EPA)
- EPA released a PFAS Strategic Roadmap (2021-2024) committing to research, control, and cleanup efforts
- EPA established lifetime health advisory levels for PFBS, GenX, PFOA, and PFOS in June 2022
- ATSDR finalized toxicological profiles and minimal risk levels for certain PFAS in May 2021
- EPA added five PFAS substances to Regional Screening and Removal Management Levels in May 2022
- EPA is developing national primary drinking water regulations for PFOA and PFOS, with proposed standards expected in early 2023
- EPA published Drinking Water Contaminant Candidates List 5 (CCL 5) in November 2022
- EPA issued guidance to states for incorporating PFAS requirements into Clean Water Act permits
- EPA proposed designating PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances and initiated RCRA rulemaking for four PFAS compounds
- EPA expanded reporting requirements under TSCA and TRI, adding over 170 PFAS compounds
- EPA issued a test order in January 2023 requiring manufacturers to conduct health and safety testing for HFPO

## State Activity
- Multiple states have established drinking water standards for PFAS (California, Vermont, New Jersey, New York, New Hampshire, Michigan, Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, Wisconsin)
- States have enacted restrictions on PFAS in consumer products (Colorado, California, Maine)
- California listed PFOS and PFOA as carcinogens and PFNA as a reproductive toxicant under Proposition 65
- Maine enacted a ban on intentionally added PFAS in products effective January 1, 2030
- 3M is involved in litigation challenging PFAS regulations in New Jersey, New York, and Michigan
- Seven states enacted laws requiring notification of PFAS in firefighting PPE, with 3M identified as non-compliant for some products

nodes.title: United States: State Activity
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Regulatory and Legislative Actions on PFAS

- EPA released draft Aquatic Life Criteria for PFOA and PFOS in April 2022, with 3M submitting comments
- Multiple states have established or are evaluating drinking water standards, cleanup standards, and groundwater values for PFOS, PFOA, and other PFAS compounds
- Nine states (California, Vermont, New Jersey, New York, New Hampshire, Michigan, Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, and Wisconsin) have finalized drinking water standards for certain PFAS
- Several states have enacted laws restricting PFAS in consumer products (Colorado, California, Maine) and requiring notification of PFAS in firefighting equipment

## 3M's Litigation and Legal Challenges

- 3M filed appeals and lawsuits challenging PFAS regulations in New Jersey, New York, and Michigan
- The Michigan case resulted in a favorable ruling for 3M in November 2022, invalidating EGLE's drinking water standards
- 3M identified non-compliance with firefighting PPE notification requirements and began remedial actions in November 2022

## Alabama PFAS Contamination Litigation

- 3M has resolved multiple claims related to PFAS contamination from its Decatur, Alabama manufacturing facility
- Major settlements include: $35 million with West Morgan-East Lawrence Water Authority, $99 million collaborative agreement with City of Decatur and Morgan County, and $30 million settlement with Guin Water Works
- Ongoing litigation includes class actions and individual property owner claims

## State Attorneys General Litigation

- Multiple state attorneys general have filed lawsuits against 3M in federal MDL court in South Carolina regarding AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam)
- Twelve states and two territories have brought lawsuits seeking injunctive relief, remediation, damages, and penalties related to PFAS contamination

nodes.title: Litigation Related to Historical PFAS Manufacturing Operations in Alabama
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section discusses 3M's litigation and regulatory matters related to PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination, covering two main areas:

## Litigation Related to Historical PFAS Manufacturing Operations in Alabama

The document details multiple lawsuits and settlements involving 3M's Decatur and Guin, Alabama manufacturing facilities:
- **Decatur facility settlements**: Including a $35 million settlement with West Morgan-East Lawrence Water & Sewer Authority (2019), class action settlements (Lindsey, St. John cases), and a $99 million collaborative agreement with the City of Decatur and Morgan County (2021)
- **Guin facility litigation**: A $30 million settlement agreement for water and wastewater treatment systems (2021), plus a subsequent federal class action filed in 2022
- **Additional claims**: Ongoing negotiations with individual property owners and settlements in various personal injury cases (King, Billings, Stover, Owens, Chandler matters)
- **Recent developments**: Colbert County's lawsuit filed in August 2022 alleging Tennessee River contamination from Decatur operations

## State Attorneys General Litigation Related to PFAS

The document outlines lawsuits filed by multiple state attorneys general in federal Multi-District Litigation (MDL) court in South Carolina regarding Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF). Twelve states and two U.S. territories have filed lawsuits against 3M seeking injunctive relief, remedial work, compensatory damages, natural resource damages, attorneys' fees, and punitive damages related to PFAS contamination response.

nodes.title: State Attorneys General Litigation related to PFAS
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers 3M's litigation and regulatory matters related to PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination, including:

## Main Points:

1. **Regulatory Uncertainty**: Discussion of unpredictable regulatory actions in the U.S., Europe, and elsewhere regarding PFAS compounds, with significant uncertainty about remediation and control technology costs.

2. **Alabama PFAS Manufacturing Litigation**: Detailed account of multiple settled and ongoing lawsuits related to 3M's Decatur and Guin, Alabama facilities, including:
   - Water authority settlements ($35 million and $30 million respectively)
   - Class action settlements (Lindsey, St. John cases)
   - Individual plaintiff settlements (King, Stover, Owens, Chandler matters)
   - Collaborative agreement with City of Decatur ($99 million contribution)
   - Ongoing property owner claims and environmental remediation efforts

3. **State Attorneys General PFAS Litigation**: Multiple lawsuits filed by state attorneys general in federal MDL court and state courts, including cases from New Jersey, New Hampshire, Vermont, Illinois, California, and other states, seeking injunctive relief, remediation costs, damages, and penalties.

4. **AFFF Environmental Litigation**: Overview of 3M's liability for Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) used in firefighting from 1963-2002, with 3,350 pending lawsuits in federal MDL court and state courts involving individuals, water systems, and municipalities seeking damages for personal injury, property damage, water treatment costs, and natural resource damages.

nodes.title: Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) Environmental Litigation
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers pending litigation involving 3M related to PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination, organized into two main categories:

## State Attorneys General Lawsuits (Outside AFFF MDL)

- **New Jersey**: Two consolidated actions filed in March 2019 against 3M, DuPont, and Chemours regarding alleged PFOA discharges at facilities in Pennsville and Parlin; currently in discovery phase
- **New Hampshire**: Two lawsuits filed in May 2019 alleging PFAS contamination of drinking water; one transferred to AFFF MDL, the other pending remand motion
- **Vermont**: Two lawsuits filed in June 2019; one in AFFF MDL, the other proceeding in state court with trial-ready date set for October 2024; complaint amended in October 2022 to add PFBS and GenX claims
- **Illinois**: Two separate lawsuits filed in March 2022 and January 2023 alleging PFAS contamination from 3M's Cordova plant and multiple drinking water systems statewide
- **California**: Lawsuit filed in November 2022 against 18 defendants including 3M; case transferred to federal court with conditional transfer order to AFFF MDL

Additional states and District of Columbia are pursuing similar litigation through retained outside law firms.

## AFFF and Other PFAS-Related Litigation

- **AFFF Cases**: As of December 31, 2022, 3,350 lawsuits pending in federal MDL in South Carolina and state courts; includes personal injury, property damage, water treatment costs, and natural resource damage claims
- **Other PFAS Cases**: Individual and class action complaints involving PFOA and PFOS contamination from products like Scotchgard, with specific cases detailed in New York, Michigan, and other jurisdictions

nodes.title: Other PFAS-related Product and Environmental Litigation
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers 3M's litigation and regulatory matters related to AFFF (aqueous film-forming foam) and PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination. The main points include:

## AFFF Litigation
- Consolidated MDL (Multi-District Litigation) proceeding in U.S. District Court for the District of South Carolina, established in December 2018
- Extensive discovery conducted over four years with bellwether trials planned (City of Stuart, Florida case scheduled for June 2023)
- Court denied defendants' summary judgment motions on government contractor defense in September 2022
- Mediation efforts ongoing with retired federal judge appointed as mediator in October 2022
- Valero Energy Corporation subsidiaries filed eight AFFF cases against 3M and other defendants; approximately 50 additional AFFF suits identified as of December 31, 2022

## PFAS-Related Product and Environmental Litigation
- Multiple lawsuits across various states (New York, Michigan, Alabama, Georgia, California, North Carolina, Delaware, New Jersey, South Carolina, Massachusetts, Maine, and Ohio) alleging contamination from 3M products containing PFOA and PFOS
- Cases involve individual claims, putative class actions, and municipal water utility lawsuits
- Plaintiffs allege improper disposal led to groundwater/surface water contamination and seek damages for personal injury, property damage, remediation costs, and medical monitoring
- Notable cases include water contamination in Hoosick Falls, NY; Summerville, GA; and various other municipalities
- Settlement reached in Michigan case with Wolverine World Wide; agreement with Gadsden Water Works in September 2022

## Other PFAS Matters
- Congressional oversight: House Subcommittee on Environment requested documents in July 2019; 3M representative testified in September 2019
- Ongoing remediation efforts at manufacturing facilities in Decatur, Alabama; Cottage Grove, Minnesota; and Cordova, Illinois under state regulatory supervision

nodes.title: Alabama and Georgia PFAS Cases
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document outlines multiple PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination lawsuits in which 3M is a defendant across several U.S. states. The main points covered include:

**Geographic Scope and Cases:**
- **Alabama and Georgia**: Municipal water utility cases involving PFAS-contaminated drinking water supplies along the Coosa River (Rome, Georgia and Centre, Alabama), with the City of Rome case scheduled for trial in June 2023. A settlement was reached with Gadsden Water Works in September 2022.
- **Georgia**: Multiple cases including individual class actions and utility board actions alleging PFAS contamination from carpet manufacturers and textile mills, with cases proceeding through discovery or intervention phases.
- **California**: Cases filed by the City of Corona and local utilities alleging PFAS contamination from 3M products and facilities, with one case transferred to an AFFF MDL (Multi-District Litigation). Additional class actions filed regarding contamination of the Temescal Subbasin.
- **North Carolina**: A January 2023 lawsuit by the town of Pittsboro alleging PFAS contamination from AFFF sources.
- **Delaware**: A putative class action involving PFAS contamination from metal plating facilities, with the negligence claim surviving dismissal.

**Case Status**: Most cases are in discovery phases, with some involving motions to dismiss, settlements, or ongoing negotiations regarding scheduling orders.

# Description of Partial Document

This document section details 3M's litigation and regulatory matters related to PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination. The main points covered include:

# Description of Partial Document

This document section details 3M's litigation and regulatory matters related to PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination across multiple jurisdictions:
